item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does the agent need to stop the summoning? Just because? A feeling? Crappy reasoning, but outside of that it's awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sounds very similar to the Nimfs invisibility helmet in Greek Myths.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Hi, sorry, author here. The photo is mine, of a gas mask I own. I'll re-add it and add whatever CC 3.0 accreditation are needed.

EDIT: Actually, I don't want to step on moderator toes here. Since it's my image, would you be willing to revert the edit you made to re-add it?

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Edit: image is user-generated

item.title: Thank you!
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot! Your SCP saved me from 106 when I was playing SCP - Containment Breach! Here's an upvote for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, Soviet issue, and seems to have led the agent to a church.

Perhaps originally designed as a part of the anti-church push during Soviet times, but meant to make it so that agents wouldn't feel weird killing fellow Russians or church leaders?

item.title: SCP 1499 Achievement
item.content:encoded:
While watching one of Nepheron's gameplays, this thought just spawned into my brain:

In game, when a player puts on SCP 1499 for the first time the player earns the achievement "Screams of the Present" and I may either be totally getting the meaning of the achievement title wrong or it should have been really obvious from the start, but is there a reference to how the player can hear 096's screaming if they put it on right after triggering 096 or right as 096 gets really close cause that's when 096's screaming can be heard but is still heard for a while after putting on the mask and the screams technically do belong to the present.

What do you people think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject found herself in building of an unknown black substance.

What?

Agent was capable of descending down the building

Seems redundant.

This entire article is an incoherent mess. The dialogue sounds unnatural and the exploration log barely makes sense. The "twist" does nothing for me. Just feels cliche.

Downvote

item.title: Re: DUCK
item.content:encoded: The real SCP is in the comments.

item.title: DUCK
item.content:encoded: I like how the names of the test subjects make an anagram for duck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why should they? Yes, he killed an innocent civilian, but it wasn't like it was on purpose. It was the product of an experiment gone wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCP, but not the ending. The ending was just WRONG! Did they even terminate the Agent for doing this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-178. That's what you mean. Except the catch about this is that SCP-178 doesn't make humans look evil. This does. However, SCP-178 does have entities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Under '+ Options', the 'Page Source' option will let you see the page's code without editing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The amount of times I pressed edit to learn how to tablecode, aaahhh…. but I didnt change anything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was made to be a gas mask,Wierd crap made it anamolous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a classic feeling to it without disappointing. Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was all set to downvote what seemed to be a very basic, spoopy, alt-dimension article; but the ending turned that right around!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost four months later, revisiting it, yup! Still just as horrifying and engaging as it was. I really love this SCP and it should easily be over +300.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was already into this before the final note after the exploration log.

Damn. I did not expect that at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverse pyrovision?

item.title: Security Clearance
item.content:encoded: This is my #1 problem with a lot of skips. If important, relevant information is only available to the O5 Council, then HOW THE HELL ARE YOU SUPPOSED TO CONTAIN IT?!? I understand why it would be important to keep D-Class and the lowly Junior Researchers out of the loop, but what about the guys who watch it for everyone's safety and the guys who watch it to see what it does? Wouldn't at least Level 3 Researchers and Level 4 Site admins need to know this stuff? It seems like you're just throwing high security clearances about to make this seem more important and scarier than it is. The O5s have 2000+ highly anomalous items and countless kinda anomalous items to look out for. They're not gonna be concerned about a gas mask that teleports people to Russia and makes them see funny. Ugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but I didn't care for the generic descriptions made by the agent in the addendum; I found it jarring that someone whose job is doing some sort of recon describes the environment as "very big" or populated with a "large amount" of creatures. I think rough number estimates would work better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic…before the addendum I thought it was ok. A little played out, but still interesting.

But then it got great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be rated at 300. I upvoted long ago and recently re-read it. Just stopped by to say that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought that this was just another cheesy item that sends you to some god awful dimension with evil murder demons and stuff. Then I read the addendum and this instantly became my favorite SCP. I tip my hat to you sir, for you have just succeeded in mentally fucking me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the concept behind this is good, but it just seemed kinda corny to me :/ I just didn't like it, really. It's solid writing, but the monsters seemed a bit arbitrary and the worm summoning almost made me cringe. I rarely dislike articles enough to downvote them, but this just makes me a bit sad, and not in the good way some writing does.

Edit: Oh Jesus, I should've read the addendum. I think I'll change that downvote, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is still awesome, and for some reason I didn't up-vote the first time I read this. Of course, I have now rectified that.
Always liked this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted! It felt kind of bland at the beginning: another item that sends you to Dimension X, but the twist at the end improved the whole thing considerably. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it very much in theory and have upvoted, but I think it could stand some style editing. The phrase "Large amount of _" is hugely overused in this entry. When describing quantity, "amount" is better suited to something imprecise, such as a mass of fluid. "A large amount of jam," for example, or "a large amount of pudding." Even then, "a significant quantity" or "a considerable mass." If you could actually count it, like number of mouths, or towers, "large number of __" is a better phrase. If only to cut down on repetition, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A gasmask that teleports you and makes you see reality.

I liked it too. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A gasmask that teleports you and makes you see crazy.

I liked it. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally like the lack of explanation or reasoning behind it. I feel like it adds to that mystery of "What was this thing made for?" was it created to disrupt the church, and cause people to destroy its leaders? Or is simply an insight into something we can't understand and simply causes us fear of the unknown, in this case the aggression.

I don't think there should be a reason behind his attack more than "I just felt like it had to be stopped."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminded me a lot of SCP-093 until the twist. Very enjoyable!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Prolonged exposure to SCP-1499 appears to affect the wearer's mental state; along with the typical visual/auditory hallucinations, the prolonged state of the world around them appearing "wrong" begins to override their typical reactions, typically caused by a singular hallucination which is described as "feeling evil" or similarly "must be stopped." That has occured in [blackbox]% of wearers beyond fifteen minutes."

That's (one possible way) how it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to go stop it.

This doesn't make any goddamn sense.

The rest of this is great, so I'm still upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would make more sense if the people were acting in a way that warranted defensiveness on the part of the Agent.

Perhaps it could play out with the Agent deciding to go toward the congregation to inspect the portal thing and maybe make contact with the SCP-1499-1 instances. But because he does so with his gun semi-drawn, the crowd freaks out, which freaks him out, which triggers the scuffle.

As for ripping out the priest's heart, I'm not sure how that would work out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a fondness for articles that look like something that's been done for death but then pull a good twist on us. This is one such article! +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, that twist was amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. Like others said, I thought it was just a 093 rip off, but that twist was wonderful.

The only thing I can think of is somehow tying in the era and place it's from. I'm sure certain people on both sides in the Cold War could have reason to produce this. The US, to distribute to citizens and induce panic, the USSR, to demonize the enemy in case of invasion.

But, hey, that's just my mental conspiracy. Tacking it on may be a bad choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT nevermind, misread the post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'll make the correction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it's a GP-5 and is actually post-WW2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very Twilight Zone, I love it. Emphatic upvote sir, will done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here they are learning about an apparently alien world and all of the sudden he decides to rip out the glowing heart of someone that hasn't attacked or even seen him?

Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, a technical issue: the image you're using is 2048 by 1536 pixels. You should use an image editor to scale it down to the desired size and then upload it.

It seems to me that one of the first things the Foundation would have their agents do would be to try to take back samples of the soil and building materials. If the samples came back unchanged, the Foundation would be able to tell that it was ordinary soil from Earth and ordinary building materials, which would make them think that maybe the SCP is sending the agents to somewhere else on Earth while altering what the agent perceives. You could change the article so that what the agents bring back is changed when the SCP is removed, to match the world that the SCP is receiving. And maybe even change it so that instead of someone in a suit attacking that Russian church, it's something that looks like the entities described by the agents (but still wearing the gas-mask) what attacked. That would leave in question if it was the agent who did the attack, or if it was one of the entities with their own copy of the SCP retaliating for what the agent did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One implication in this SCP that I like and no one has mentioned: the agent saw the congregation singing hymns as monsters, and the priest as a terrible monster leader. However, what was the giant worm that he saw? If it was truly the Judeo-Christian God, than that implies that it not only exists but is under the thrall of this SCP, same as us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it enough to upvote, but I think the sudden decision of the agent to engage and kill is jarring. Here they are learning about an apparently alien world and all of the sudden he decides to rip out the glowing heart of someone that hasn't attacked or even seen him? I think that action, although critical to your article, needs more justification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Defuse the situation", not "diffuse".

Upvoted anyway. I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent K█████: "Yeah, it's big. Very big. There aren't many structures around it. It is quite elaborate; I see a large amount of towers and spikes. There looks like there's blood on it."

That's what she said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this is what Pyro sees when Pyrovision goggles overlap his natural Pyrovision?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn it. I…

…God damn it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhhh… it reads as if someone read 093 in the middle of playing Oblivion, and the ending is too Twilight Zone for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you believe in magic in a young girl's heart…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The twist ending was very nice. Initially I was going to ask why it had to be a human heart, but the next paragraph was golden. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At fisrt i was "this is 093 but boring" then i clicked on the collapsible. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was "meh" on it until the ending. Nicely done. +1
